Source material (consume this first): /tmp/ddx-axon-backend-plan.md — full integration plan with Axon's external interface mapped, schema/operation/concurrency mapping spelled out field-by-field, migration plan, and locked operator decisions.

LOCKED DECISIONS (do not relitigate; bake into TD):
1. Deployment: separate axon-server, operator-managed. DDx does NOT spawn or supervise Axon. Document the .ddx/config.yaml shape + ddx doctor diagnostic.
2. Auth: localhost-only by default; ts-net for remote. Reuse ADR-006 tsnet integration.
3. Events storage: separate ddx_bead_events collection with event_of links (Option B). Two collections in Axon.
4. Offline posture: refuse all bead ops when Axon unreachable. No local cache, no WAL.

Defaults the TD can adopt without rechecking with operator (revisit only if implementation surfaces a problem):
- Schema versioning: lazy migrate on read
- ddx bead ready query: two-phase (status filter + per-candidate traverse) with benchmark-then-optimize escalation path
- Backup/DR: operator-managed via Axon's own backing-store DR; ddx bead export is the logical backup format

Open questions for the Axon team (file as cross-repo coordination if needed):
- Is there a server-side aggregation/computed-view for the ready-queue traversal pattern?
- Does Axon's ts-net story align with ADR-006 (Tailscale tsnet listener) or require something different?

Output: TD-<NNN>-axon-bead-backend.md with sections for: schema mapping (DDx bead → Axon entity); operation mapping (DDx CLI → Axon RPC); deployment + config; concurrency model; migration plan; test plan. depends_on: ADR-004, SD-004, FEAT-004.

If `doc` is the existing noun, this is arguably fine, but the AC should be reconciled. The `Aliases: []string{\"integrity\"}` alias doesn't cover the spec'd names.\n**AC 7 — `--json` exit code**: `cli/cmd/doc.go:79-83` — when `--json` is set, the command encodes issues and returns `nil` unconditionally. The AC requires exit 1 when issues are present regardless of output format, so CI pipelines using `ddx doc audit --json` would incorrectly report success on a broken graph. The test `TestDocAuditCommand_JSONOutput` even documents this as intentional (\"--json always succeeds (exit 0) so piping into jq works\"), which contradicts the AC. Either the AC should be amended or the `--json` path should also return `ExitError` when issues exist (with a `--exit-zero` escape hatch for pipe-friendly use).\nresult_rev=61a8029103abff2e64442ff7b81ab92d04d84f69\nbase_rev=48f9750c2e18e96af665670c653a8f4f72e27ebf","created_at":"2026-04-23T02:38:22.039899928Z","kind":"execute-bead","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"review_request_changes"}

The file is tracked\n(`git ls-files` confirms) and present in the worktree.\n\nTD-025 covers all three items called out in the bead description:\n\n1. Pairing rule\n   Section \"Pairing Rule\" specifies append-not-replace\n   (foo.png ↔ foo.png.ddx.yaml, diagram.excalidraw ↔\n   diagram.excalidraw.ddx.yaml). Same-directory requirement,\n   case-sensitivity, glob discovery (`**/*.ddx.yaml`), and rationale\n   for append-vs-replace are all documented.\n\n2. Conflict rule (frontmatter + sidecar)\n   Section \"Conflict Rule\" sets the strict policy: markdown uses\n   embedded frontmatter exclusively, non-markdown uses sidecars\n   exclusively. A `*.md.ddx.yaml` sidecar alongside a `*.md` file is a\n   validation error; sidecar is ignored to avoid duplicate IDs. Full\n   conflict table with outcomes is provided.\n\n3. Orphan-check participation in `ddx doc validate`\n   Section \"Orphan-Check Participation\" specifies parity with markdown\n   artifacts, the role of `generated_by` edges in suppressing orphan\n   warnings (provenance-only but does count for orphan suppression),\n   and the distinct \"dangling sidecar\" hard-error rule for sidecars\n   without paired assets. Validation summary table at the end\n   enumerates every check and severity.\n\nWhat is blocking\n----------------\nNothing is blocking; there is no further work for this bead. The\nprior review note (\"REVIEW:BLOCK — diff contains only execution\nmanifest/result metadata; the actual sidecar schema design document\nreferenced as cda66189 in result_rev is not part of the changed\nfiles\") appears to have inspected the execution-metadata diff rather\nthan the merged source tree. The artifact named in result_rev is\npresent in the repository at HEAD.\n\nWhat a follow-up attempt would need\n-----------------------------------\nA follow-up attempt should not regenerate or rewrite TD-025 — that\nwould churn already-approved design content and risk diverging from\nthe consumers of the schema (FEAT-005 / FEAT-007 scanner refactor in\nlater beads). If the reviewer believes specific gaps remain, those\ngaps should be enumerated as concrete acceptance items\n(e.g., \"specify behavior when sidecar YAML is malformed\", \"define\nsidecar schema-version field\") so the next attempt has a precise\ndelta to address. Re-queuing on the same description will produce\nthe same conclusion.",

.agents/skills/website/static/demos/01-install.cast:18:[4.738505, "o", "Usage:\r\n  ddx [command]\r\n\r\nAvailable Commands:\r\n  agent       Invoke AI agents with harness dispatch, quorum, and session logging\r\n  bead        Manage beads (portable work items)\r\n  checkpoint  Create, list, or restore named git checkpoints\r\n  completion  Generate completion script\r\n  config      Configure DDx settings\r\n  doc         Document dependency graph and staleness tracking\r\n  doctor      Check installation health and diagnose issues\r\n  exec        Validate and run DDx execution definitions\r\n  help        Help about any command\r\n  init        Initialize DDx in current project\r\n  install     Install a package, resource, or the embedded DDx skills\r\n  installed   List installed packages\r\n  jq          Process JSON with jq expressions\r\n  list        List available DDx resources\r\n  log         Show DDX asset history\r\n  metric      Validate and run metric definitions\r\n  metrics     Show process metrics derived from beads and agent sessions\r\n  outdated    List installed packages with available updates\r\n  persona     Manage AI personas for consistent interactions\r\n  prompts     Manage AI prompts\r\n  search      Search available packages\r\n  server      Start the DDx HTTP and MCP server\r\n  skills      Validate bundled skill packages\r\n  status      Show version and status information\r\n  uninstall   Remove an installed package\r\n  update      Update DDx CLI or installed plugins\r\n  upgrade     Upgrade DDx to the latest version\r\n  verify      Verify installed package integrity\r\n  version     Show version information\r\n  work        Work the bead execution queue\r\n\r\nFlags:\r\n      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)\r\n  -h, --help                       help for ddx\r\n      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location\r\n  -v, --verbose                    verbose output\r\n\r\nUse \"ddx [command] --help\" for more information about a command.\r\n"]

If\\n  `--provider vidar-omlx` does not match any entry in `builtinHarnesses`, the\\n  value is silently discarded.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:318` — `flags.Model != \\\"\\\"` branch routes the\\n  model through `catalog.NormalizeModelRef` to produce either `ModelRef` (known\\n  in catalog) or `ModelPin` (exact, bypasses catalog policy).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/types.go:337` — `RouteRequest` struct definition. silently skips unreachable ones (partial failure is tolerated).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:162` — cache write under mutex.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:170` — `InvalidateDiscoveryCache` is the only\\n  way to force a re-probe in tests.\\n\\nDiscovery is invoked on the routing hot-path only when a `ModelPin` is not in\\nthe catalog:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:576` — in `ProbeAndBuildCandidatePlans`, the\\n  discovery probe fires when `req.ModelPin != \\\"\\\" && req.ModelRef == \\\"\\\" && r.Discovery == nil`.\\n\\nDiscovery is invoked a second time on the dispatch path:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:521` — in `resolveNativeAgentProvider`,\\n  `r.Discovery.ProvidersForModel(model)` looks up which providers advertised\\n  the requested model.\\n\\n## Layer 4 — Candidate planning\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:27` — `BuildCandidatePlans(req, stateOverride)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:33` through `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:46` — iterates every registered harness. `TestOnly` harnesses (script, virtual) are skipped unless `req.HarnessOverride` names them explicitly — this is the guard that prevents `script` from leaking into standard-tier fallback chains (see ddx-869848ec in the comment on line 40).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:47` — calls `evaluateCandidate` per harness.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:106` — `evaluateCandidate(name, harness, req, stateOverride)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:396` — `rejectIfNotViable(plan, req)` runs the\\n  viability gate. Checks:\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:399` — `!s.Installed`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:402` — `!s.Reachable`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:405` — `!s.Authenticated`.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:408` — quota exhausted.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:411` — degraded.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:414` — policy violation.\\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:418` — harness override mismatch.\\n\\nNote: none of these check *capability* — context window, tool support, effort\\nlevel support. That is Gap #6.\\n\\n## Layer 5 — Fuzzy matching\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:66` — `FuzzyMatchModel(input)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:70` — delegates to `fuzzyMatchInPool` using\\n  `d.AllModels()` (a flat deduplicated pool across all providers — not per-provider).\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:76` — `fuzzyMatchInPool(input, pool)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:78` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:82` — exact-match short-circuit.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:89` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:96` — prefix match: candidate `m` is considered if `len(m) > len(input) && m[:len(input)] == input`. **The comparison is case-sensitive and treats slashes literally (Gap #3).**\\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:100` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:106` — tiebreak: shortest suffix wins; alphabetical on equal suffix length. **Not version-aware (Gap #5).**\\n\\n**Concrete outcome for our invocation:**\\nPool likely contains `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit`. Input `qwen/qwen3.6` fails the\\nexact check, then fails the prefix check because `Q != q` and the slash is not\\nin the pool entries. The function returns `(\\\"\\\", false)`. `resolveNativeAgentProvider`\\nfalls through to the `isOpenRouterModel` heuristic at\\n`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:544` (vendor/model format → openrouter),\\nwhich is not what the operator wanted.\\n\\n## Layer 6 — Scoring\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:477` — `scoreCandidate(profile, plan)` entry.\\n  Returns a `float64`; higher is better.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:478` — base score `100`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:485` — `switch profile` over `cheap`, `standard`, `smart`, default.\\n  - `cheap` (lines 486–493): `local` +40, within-quota subscription +20, penalize `cr * 30`.\\n  - `standard` (lines 495–502): `local` +25, within-quota +15, penalize `cr * 10`.\\n  - `smart` (lines 504–512): `cr * 20` bonus (expensive > cheap), within-quota +5.\\n  - default (lines 514–522): treated as standard.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:525` — quota >80%: penalty scales with over-usage.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:528` — quota state unknown: −3.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:531` — routing-signal freshness: `cached` −1, `stale` −4.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:542` — historical success rate (≥3 samples):\\n  ≥0.8 +20, <0.5 −30.\\n\\n**No provider-affinity bonus (Gap #4).** If two harnesses both report viable\\nfor the same requested model, `scoreCandidate` does not look at\\n`plan.Provider` vs the provider the model was discovered on.\\n\\n## Layer 7 — Candidate ranking and selection\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:434` — `RankCandidates(profile, plans)`.\\n  - Assigns scores (line 444) or `-1` for non-viable (line 441).\\n  - Stable sort (lines 448–466): viable before non-viable; descending score;\\n    tiebreak prefers local-cost candidates, then alphabetical harness name.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:556` — `SelectBestCandidate(plans)` returns\\n  the first viable plan, or `\\\"no viable harness candidate: all harnesses rejected\\\"`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:567` — `ProbeAndBuildCandidatePlans` is the\\n  full-probe wrapper used by actual dispatch (it calls live `ProbeHarnessState`\\n  per harness before building plans).\\n\\n## Layer 8 — Dispatch\\n\\n`Runner.Run` is the dispatch fork:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:141` — `Run(opts RunOptions) (*Result, error)` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:148` — `resolveHarness(opts)` picks the harness.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:376` — `resolveHarness(opts)` definition.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:382` — **Gap #2 origin**: if `opts.Harness == \\\"\\\"` and\\n  `opts.Provider != \\\"\\\"`, the `--provider` value is looked up as a harness\\n  alias. The comment on lines 185–186 references `ddx-501e87ef` — the bug where cheap-tier attempts were burning <1s with \\\"exec: no command\\\".\\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:278` — default exec path: `Executor.ExecuteInDir(ctx, harness.Binary, args, stdin, execDir)`.\\n\\nEmbedded agent dispatch:\\n\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:65` — `RunAgent(opts)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:94` — `resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider(wd, model, opts.Provider, opts.ModelRef)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:464` — `resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider` entry.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:493` — `resolveNativeAgentProvider(workDir, model, explicitProvider, explicitModelRef)`.\\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:519` — **Gap #2 again**: `explicitProvider != \\\"\\\"` overrides the default provider with the raw `opts.Provider` string. .ddx/executions/20260423T041559-0a437c34/manifest.json:14:    "acceptance": "**User story:** As a developer opening the document graph, I want a structured integrity panel that groups real document problems by kind and points me at the files to fix, so I can triage a noisy graph quickly and confirm when it is healthy.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. `docgraph.Graph.Warnings` (or a sibling `Issues` field) is a typed slice with `Kind`, `Path`, `ID`, `Message`, and optional `RelatedPath`. Kinds include at minimum: `duplicate_id`, `parse_error`, `missing_dep`, `id_path_missing`, `id_path_mismatch`, `required_root_missing`, `cascade_unknown`.\n2. GraphQL schema exposes `DocGraph.issues: [GraphIssue!]!` with the same fields. The existing string `warnings` either stays (rendered from issues) or is deprecated with a schema comment.\n3. Unit tests in `cli/internal/docgraph/docgraph_test.go` cover each issue kind: fixtures that produce exactly one instance of that kind and assert the typed output.\n4. `/graph` page renders an **Integrity** panel when `issues.length \u003e 0`:\n   - grouped by `kind` with a count per group;\n   - each row shows path, id, and message;\n   - path links to the document viewer (reuses existing documents route);\n   - total-issues badge appears next to the page title.\n5. With zero issues, neither the panel nor the badge renders, and the graph view is visually identical to a healthy graph today.\n6. Duplicate-ID rows show both paths and a \"copy suggested unique ID\" button; the suggestion is deterministic (same input → same output) and a unit test asserts the suggestion function's stability.\n7. `ddx docs audit` (or `ddx doctor docs`) CLI command prints the same issue list grouped by kind, exits 0 if empty and 1 if any issues present. Integration test asserts exit codes and grouped output.\n8. Playwright e2e:\n   - navigates to `/graph` on a fixture repo with one seeded duplicate-id pair and one missing-dep target;\n   - asserts the Integrity panel shows groups \"Duplicate ID (1)\" and \"Missing dep target (1)\";\n   - expands the Duplicate ID group and asserts both paths are visible;\n   - clicks the first path and asserts navigation to the documents route for that file;\n   - on a clean fixture repo, asserts neither the panel nor the badge is present.\n\n**Dependency:** best sequenced after `ddx-12cae4dd` lands so the fixture repos are not polluted by `.claude/worktrees` noise, but implementation does not block on it — the two can proceed in parallel and this bead's tests use synthetic fixtures.",

.ddx/executions/20260423T041559-0a437c34/prompt.md:8:**User story:** As a developer opening the document graph, I want a structured integrity panel that groups real document problems by kind and points me at the files to fix, so I can triage a noisy graph quickly and confirm when it is healthy.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. `docgraph.Graph.Warnings` (or a sibling `Issues` field) is a typed slice with `Kind`, `Path`, `ID`, `Message`, and optional `RelatedPath`. Kinds include at minimum: `duplicate_id`, `parse_error`, `missing_dep`, `id_path_missing`, `id_path_mismatch`, `required_root_missing`, `cascade_unknown`.
2. GraphQL schema exposes `DocGraph.issues: [GraphIssue!]!` with the same fields. The existing string `warnings` either stays (rendered from issues) or is deprecated with a schema comment.
3. Unit tests in `cli/internal/docgraph/docgraph_test.go` cover each issue kind: fixtures that produce exactly one instance of that kind and assert the typed output.
4. `/graph` page renders an **Integrity** panel when `issues.length > 0`:
   - grouped by `kind` with a count per group;
   - each row shows path, id, and message;
   - path links to the document viewer (reuses existing documents route);
   - total-issues badge appears next to the page title.
5. With zero issues, neither the panel nor the badge renders, and the graph view is visually identical to a healthy graph today.
6. Duplicate-ID rows show both paths and a "copy suggested unique ID" button; the suggestion is deterministic (same input → same output) and a unit test asserts the suggestion function's stability.
7. `ddx docs audit` (or `ddx doctor docs`) CLI command prints the same issue list grouped by kind, exits 0 if empty and 1 if any issues present. Integration test asserts exit codes and grouped output.
8. Playwright e2e:
   - navigates to `/graph` on a fixture repo with one seeded duplicate-id pair and one missing-dep target;
   - asserts the Integrity panel shows groups "Duplicate ID (1)" and "Missing dep target (1)";
   - expands the Duplicate ID group and asserts both paths are visible;
   - clicks the first path and asserts navigation to the documents route for that file;
   - on a clean fixture repo, asserts neither the panel nor the badge is present.

**Dependency:** best sequenced after `ddx-12cae4dd` lands so the fixture repos are not polluted by `.claude/worktrees` noise, but implementation does not block on it — the two can proceed in parallel and this bead's tests use synthetic fixtures.
.ddx/executions/20260423T041559-0a437c34/prompt.md:11:**AC 7 — command path**: The command is registered as `ddx doc audit` (`cli/cmd/doc.go:59`), but the acceptance criteria specifies `ddx docs audit` (or `ddx doctor docs`). If `doc` is the existing noun, this is arguably fine, but the AC should be reconciled. The `Aliases: []string{"integrity"}` alias doesn't cover the spec'd names.
**AC 7 — `--json` exit code**: `cli/cmd/doc.go:79-83` — when `--json` is set, the command encodes issues and returns `nil` unconditionally. The AC requires exit 1 when issues are present regardless of output format, so CI pipelines using `ddx doc audit --json` would incorrectly report success on a broken graph. The test `TestDocAuditCommand_JSONOutput` even documents this as intentional ("--json always succeeds (exit 0) so piping into jq works"), which contradicts the AC. Either the AC should be amended or the `--json` path should also return `ExitError` when issues exist (with a `--exit-zero` escape hatch for pipe-friendly use).

`cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/graph.spec.ts:177` — AC 8 asks for a Playwright e2e on a fixture repo with one seeded duplicate-id pair and one missing-dep target. The test directly injects `duplicate_id` and `missing_dep` objects through a mocked `/graphql` response, so it does not exercise docgraph detection, GraphQL resolver plumbing, or project fixture routing end-to-end.

.ddx/executions/20260423T025034-5febdfb4/prompt.md:11:**AC 7 — command path**: The command is registered as `ddx doc audit` (`cli/cmd/doc.go:59`), but the acceptance criteria specifies `ddx docs audit` (or `ddx doctor docs`). If `doc` is the existing noun, this is arguably fine, but the AC should be reconciled. The `Aliases: []string{"integrity"}` alias doesn't cover the spec'd names.
**AC 7 — `--json` exit code**: `cli/cmd/doc.go:79-83` — when `--json` is set, the command encodes issues and returns `nil` unconditionally. The AC requires exit 1 when issues are present regardless of output format, so CI pipelines using `ddx doc audit --json` would incorrectly report success on a broken graph. The test `TestDocAuditCommand_JSONOutput` even documents this as intentional ("--json always succeeds (exit 0) so piping into jq works"), which contradicts the AC. Either the AC should be amended or the `--json` path should also return `ExitError` when issues exist (with a `--exit-zero` escape hatch for pipe-friendly use).

.ddx/executions/20260414T082148-b6ff5c97/manifest.json:14:    "description": "Today when execute-bead exits with no_changes, the operator sees 'agent made no commits' and nothing else. That's useless for triage — the operator cannot tell whether:\\n- the bead is genuinely already satisfied (work present in a prior commit)\\n- the agent gave up because it couldn't find the relevant files\\n- the agent classified the task as ambiguous or too risky\\n- the agent hit a tool limit or context cap\\n- the prompt contract was unclear\\n\\nThe fix is to make no_changes a structured result that carries a short rationale from the agent itself, written at the point where the agent decided not to commit. Example of what the operator should see:\\n\\n  status:    no_changes\\n  rationale: The epic filter requested by this bead was already added in\\n             commit 1da6495 (cli/internal/bead/store.go readyFiltered). I\\n             reviewed the current code and tests, confirmed the filter\\n             excludes 'epic' beads from ReadyExecution, and verified\\n             TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics exists and passes. Nothing\\n             to do. Ref: 1da6495, TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics.\\n\\nThis is specifically the case that motivated this bead: a continuous-drain worker processed ddx-b44ca394 ('Prevent execute-loop from launching open epics by default') after the epic filter had already been implemented on main. The agent instead made a synthesized no-op commit that only retargeted 17 .claude/skills/helix-* symlinks, which the loop classified as 'success' because a commit was produced. Had no_changes carried a rationale pointing at the prior commit, the loop could have closed the bead as 'already_satisfied' and moved on cleanly.\\n\\nScope:\\n1. Execute-bead prompt contract must explicitly instruct the agent: if you determine the bead's work is already present or cannot be done, report that in a structured way — do NOT make a synthesized commit to 'land something'. Today the prompt says 'Making no commits (no_changes) should be rare' which actively pushes agents toward the wrong behavior.\\n2. The agent's exit mechanism needs a way to carry a rationale string back. Simplest version: the agent writes a file to the worktree like .ddx/executions/\u003cattempt-id\u003e/no_changes_rationale.txt before exiting. ExecuteBead reads it if no commits were produced and attaches the text to the result.NoChangesRationale field.\\n3. The ExecuteBeadResult struct gains a NoChangesRationale string field, populated only when outcome == no_changes. Status: success and the unused 'reason' field need not change.\\n4. execute-loop close semantics: when no_changes arrives with a non-empty rationale pointing at a prior commit, the loop can optionally close the bead as already_satisfied with the rationale recorded in the close event body (instead of waiting for MaxNoChangesBeforeClose=3 strikes). Gate this behind a 'rationale references existing commit' heuristic to avoid flapping — if the rationale is a vague 'nothing to do', keep current cooldown behavior.\\n5. ddx server worker log should render the rationale prominently, not bury it in session metadata.\\n6. The prompt contract note that no_changes should be 'rare' is replaced with: 'If the work is already present, say so and provide a reference (commit SHA, test name, file path). A well-justified no_changes is always preferred to a cosmetic synthesized commit.'",

That's useless for triage — the operator cannot tell whether:\n- the bead is genuinely already satisfied (work present in a prior commit)\n- the agent gave up because it couldn't find the relevant files\n- the agent classified the task as ambiguous or too risky\n- the agent hit a tool limit or context cap\n- the prompt contract was unclear\n\nThe fix is to make no_changes a structured result that carries a short rationale from the agent itself, written at the point where the agent decided not to commit. Example of what the operator should see:\n\n  status:    no_changes\n  rationale: The epic filter requested by this bead was already added in\n             commit 1da6495 (cli/internal/bead/store.go readyFiltered). I\n             reviewed the current code and tests, confirmed the filter\n             excludes 'epic' beads from ReadyExecution, and verified\n             TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics exists and passes. Nothing\n             to do. Ref: 1da6495, TestReadyExecutionExcludesEpics.\n\nThis is specifically the case that motivated this bead: a continuous-drain worker processed ddx-b44ca394 ('Prevent execute-loop from launching open epics by default') after the epic filter had already been implemented on main. The agent instead made a synthesized no-op commit that only retargeted 17 .claude/skills/helix-* symlinks, which the loop classified as 'success' because a commit was produced. Had no_changes carried a rationale pointing at the prior commit, the loop could have closed the bead as 'already_satisfied' and moved on cleanly.\n\nScope:\n1. Execute-bead prompt contract must explicitly instruct the agent: if you determine the bead's work is already present or cannot be done, report that in a structured way — do NOT make a synthesized commit to 'land something'. Today the prompt says 'Making no commits (no_changes) should be rare' which actively pushes agents toward the wrong behavior.\n2. The agent's exit mechanism needs a way to carry a rationale string back. Simplest version: the agent writes a file to the worktree like .ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/no_changes_rationale.txt before exiting. ExecuteBead reads it if no commits were produced and attaches the text to the result.NoChangesRationale field.\n3. The ExecuteBeadResult struct gains a NoChangesRationale string field, populated only when outcome == no_changes. Status: success and the unused 'reason' field need not change.\n4. execute-loop close semantics: when no_changes arrives with a non-empty rationale pointing at a prior commit, the loop can optionally close the bead as already_satisfied with the rationale recorded in the close event body (instead of waiting for MaxNoChangesBeforeClose=3 strikes). Gate this behind a 'rationale references existing commit' heuristic to avoid flapping — if the rationale is a vague 'nothing to do', keep current cooldown behavior.\n5. ddx server worker log should render the rationale prominently, not bury it in session metadata.\n6. The prompt contract note that no_changes should be 'rare' is replaced with: 'If the work is already present, say so and provide a reference (commit SHA, test name, file path). A well-justified no_changes is always preferred to a cosmetic synthesized commit.'

1	---
     2	ddx:
     3	  id: TD-035
     4	  depends_on:
     5	    - TD-026
     6	    - TD-029
     7	  status: draft
     8	---
     9	# Technical Design: Artifact Search Semantics (title -> path -> description -> frontmatter -> body)
    10	
    11	## Status
    12	
    13	Draft. This TD closes the gap left by TD-029 by defining the exact
    14	artifact-search ladder, the body-search guardrails, and the benchmark
    15	budget that govern when the resolver may broaden beyond metadata-only
    16	search.
    17	
    18	## Motivation
    19	
    20	Artifact search is the primary discovery path for documents and
    21	sidecars, but the current design trail splits the contract across TD-029
    22	and resolver code. TD-029 reserves `description`, `frontmatter`, and
    23	`body` as future search fields ([TD-029:112-129]) and already names the
    24	benchmark fixture and p95 budget ([TD-029:185-220]), while the resolver
    25	implements a body scan path and binary/size guards
    26	([resolver_artifacts.go:167-175], [resolver_artifacts.go:335-453]) with
    27	no standalone design artifact spelling out the rules. This TD makes the
    28	search order and scan limits explicit so body search is gated by a
    29	published contract instead of being an implicit implementation detail.
    30	
    31	## Search Ladder
    32	
    33	Search evaluation is ordered and short-circuited. The first field that
    34	matches admits the artifact; later fields are not consulted.
    35	
    36	1. `title`
    37	2. `path`
    38	3. `description`
    39	4. `frontmatter`
    40	5. `body`
    41	
    42	The first four levels are metadata-only and must remain cheap. `body`
    43	search is the last resort and is only consulted after all higher levels
    44	fail.
    45	
    46	## Body-Search Rules
    47	
    48	Body search is allowed only when the resolver can bound the scan.
    49	
    50	### Size cap
    51	
    52	- Per-file body scan cap: `256 KiB` (`262144` bytes).
    53	- The resolver reads at most the cap and never scans bytes beyond it.
    54	- Oversize files may still match through `title`, `path`,
    55	  `description`, or `frontmatter`; only the body portion past the cap is
    56	  excluded from search.
    57	
    58	### Binary handling
    59	
    60	Binary-like files are skipped before body scanning. A file is skipped
    61	when either condition is true:
    62	
    63	- The path extension is in the binary allowlist used by the resolver:
    64	  `.png .jpg .jpeg .gif .webp .ico .pdf .zip .tar .gz .bz2 .xz .7z
    65	  .mp3 .mp4 .mov .avi .webm .wasm .so .dylib .dll .exe .bin .o .a
    66	  .class .jar .ttf .otf .woff .woff2`
    67	- The first `512` bytes contain a NUL byte.
    68	
    69	The following formats remain text and are searchable by body:
    70	
    71	- `.svg`
    72	- `.excalidraw`
    73	- `.excalidraw.json`
    74	
    75	These are intentionally treated as text because their search value is
    76	in their labels, captions, and embedded annotations.
    77	
    78	## Performance Budget
    79	
    80	The reference benchmark is `BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture` in
    81	`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts_bench_test.go`.
    82	
    83	- Fixture mix: 200 small markdown files, 100 medium markdown files, 50
    84	  large markdown files, 25 oversize markdown files, 75 PNG sidecars, and
    85	  50 tarball sidecars.
    86	- Budget: p95 `q`-only search latency must stay within `200 ms` on the
    87	  reference dev hardware used by TD-029.
    88	- The benchmark must continue to report p50, p95, and p99 so regressions
    89	  are visible when the budget is reviewed.
    90	
    91	The budget is intended to protect the linear scan path while body search
    92	remains in-process. If a future index replaces the scan, the benchmark
    93	stays as the compatibility baseline until a new TD supersedes it.
    94	
    95	## Implementation Boundary
    96	
    97	This TD authorizes the search ladder and scan limits only. It does not
    98	change URL facets, grouping axes, or artifact identity rules, and it
    99	does not introduce a new index backend.
   100	
   101	## Non-Scope
   102	
   103	- No UI changes.
   104	- No new filter axes.
   105	- No change to artifact identity or graph schema.
   106	- No bleve / sqlite-FTS migration in this document.

-# Technical Design: Artifact Search Semantics (title -> path -> description -> frontmatter -> body)
-
-## Status
-
-Draft. This TD closes the gap left by TD-029 by defining the exact
-artifact-search ladder, the body-search guardrails, and the benchmark
-budget that govern when the resolver may broaden beyond metadata-only
-search.
-
-## Motivation
-
-Artifact search is the primary discovery path for documents and
-sidecars, but the current design trail splits the contract across TD-029
-and resolver code. TD-029 reserves `description`, `frontmatter`, and
-`body` as future search fields ([TD-029:112-129]) and already names the
-benchmark fixture and p95 budget ([TD-029:185-220]), while the resolver
-implements a body scan path and binary/size guards
-([resolver_artifacts.go:167-175], [resolver_artifacts.go:335-453]) with
-no standalone design artifact spelling out the rules. This TD makes the
-search order and scan limits explicit so body search is gated by a
-published contract instead of being an implicit implementation detail.
-
-## Search Ladder
-
-Search evaluation is ordered and short-circuited. The first field that
-matches admits the artifact; later fields are not consulted.
-
-1. `title`
-2. `path`
-3. `description`
-4. `frontmatter`
-5. `body`
-
-The first four levels are metadata-only and must remain cheap. `body`
-search is the last resort and is only consulted after all higher levels
-fail.
-
-## Body-Search Rules
-
-Body search is allowed only when the resolver can bound the scan.
-
-### Size cap
-
-- Per-file body scan cap: `256 KiB` (`262144` bytes).
-- The resolver reads at most the cap and never scans bytes beyond it.
-- Oversize files may still match through `title`, `path`,
-  `description`, or `frontmatter`; only the body portion past the cap is
-  excluded from search.
-
-### Binary handling
-
-Binary-like files are skipped before body scanning. A file is skipped
-when either condition is true:
-
-- The path extension is in the binary allowlist used by the resolver:
-  `.png .jpg .jpeg .gif .webp .ico .pdf .zip .tar .gz .bz2 .xz .7z
-  .mp3 .mp4 .mov .avi .webm .wasm .so .dylib .dll .exe .bin .o .a
-  .class .jar .ttf .otf .woff .woff2`
-- The first `512` bytes contain a NUL byte.
-
-The following formats remain text and are searchable by body:
-
-- `.svg`
-- `.excalidraw`
-- `.excalidraw.json`
-
-These are intentionally treated as text because their search value is
-in their labels, captions, and embedded annotations.
-
-## Performance Budget
-
-The reference benchmark is `BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture` in
-`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts_bench_test.go`.
-
-- Fixture mix: 200 small markdown files, 100 medium markdown files, 50
-  large markdown files, 25 oversize markdown files, 75 PNG sidecars, and
-  50 tarball sidecars.
-- Budget: p95 `q`-only search latency must stay within `200 ms` on the
-  reference dev hardware used by TD-029.
-- The benchmark must continue to report p50, p95, and p99 so regressions
-  are visible when the budget is reviewed.
-
-The budget is intended to protect the linear scan path while body search
-remains in-process. If a future index replaces the scan, the benchmark
-stays as the compatibility baseline until a new TD supersedes it.
-
-## Implementation Boundary
-
-This TD authorizes the search ladder and scan limits only. It does not
-change URL facets, grouping axes, or artifact identity rules, and it
-does not introduce a new index backend.
-
-## Non-Scope
-
-- No UI changes.
-- No new filter axes.
-- No change to artifact identity or graph schema.
-- No bleve / sqlite-FTS migration in this document.
